Benchmark Results for gemini-2.5-flash-MaxReasoning

Test 1: Travelling Salesman Problem Solver (C++) Score: 3.50 / 21
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY number of cities from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 10-40 cities (brute-force feasible for smallest cases)
- **Medium**: 100-1000 cities (requires heuristics)
- **Large**: 5000-100000 cities (requires efficient heuristics)
- **Extreme**: 500000-5000000 cities (requires very fast approximations)
- **Epic**: 10000000+ cities (requires highly optimized approximations)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with city counts ranging from 10 to 10000000. The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N (number of cities)
Lines 2..N+1: x y coordinates (floats)

**Output format (stdout):**
One line with N integers: the visit order of city indices (0-indexed), starting at city 0.
Do NOT include returning to city 0 (return is implicit).

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on problem size
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 300 seconds even for 1000,000,000 cities
3. **Quality**: Should produce reasonable routes at all scales
4. **Correctness**: Output must be a valid permutation of 0..N-1 starting with 0

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function that reads stdin and writes stdout.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on N.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: 10 Cities
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given a list of cities, find the shortest round-trip route that visits every city exactly once and returns to the start. This is one of the most famous hard problems in computer science — checking every possible route is only feasible for a handful of cities, so the AI must use clever shortcuts to find a good route quickly.

Subpasses scale from 10 cities up to 1 000, so brute-force is impossible for the larger cases. The AI's route length is compared against a simple baseline; beating it earns full marks.

 1.0000
[10 cities] Route distance: 2449.07, Baseline: 2922.93, Ratio: 0.84x, Time: 0.1s - excellent (within 10% of baseline)

TSP Solver - 10 Cities

View Code (8205 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm> // For std::min, std::swap, std::nth_element, std::sort
#include <cmath>     // For std::sqrt
#include <limits>    // For std::numeric_limits

// Define Point structure
struct Point {
    float x, y;
    int id; // Original index
};

// Function to calculate squared Euclidean distance
float distSq(const Point& p1, const Point& p2) {
    float dx = p1.x - p2.x;
    float dy = p1.y - p2.y;
    return dx * dx + dy * dy;
}

// =======================================================
// Small N Solver (Nearest Neighbor + 2-Opt)
// For N <= 500 (adjustable threshold)
// =======================================================
void solveSmallN(const std::vector<Point>& all_cities, std::vector<int>& path) {
    int N = all_cities.size();
    path.clear();
    path.reserve(N);

    std::vector<bool> visited(N, false);
    
    // Start at city 0
    int current_city_idx = 0; // Index in the all_cities vector
    path.push_back(all_cities[0].id); // Original ID of city 0
    visited[0] = true;

    // Build initial tour using Nearest Neighbor heuristic
    for (int count = 1; count < N; ++count) {
        float min_dist_sq = std::numeric_limits<float>::max();
        int next_city_idx_in_all_cities = -1;

        for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
            if (!visited[i]) {
                float d_sq = distSq(all_cities[current_city_idx], all_cities[i]);
                if (d_sq < min_dist_sq) {
                    min_dist_sq = d_sq;
                    next_city_idx_in_all_cities = i;
                }
            }
        }
        path.push_back(all_cities[next_city_idx_in_all_cities].id);
        visited[next_city_idx_in_all_cities] = true;
        current_city_idx = next_city_idx_in_all_cities;
    }

    // 2-Opt improvement
    bool improved = true;
    int iterations = 0;
    int MAX_2OPT_ITERATIONS = 5; // Limit iterations for small N

    while (improved && iterations < MAX_2OPT_ITERATIONS) {
        improved = false;
        // i and j are indices in the 'path' vector
        for (int i = 0; i < N - 1; ++i) { 
            for (int j = i + 2; j < N; ++j) { // j must be at least i+2 for a valid segment to reverse
                // Original edges: (path[i], path[i+1]) and (path[j], path[(j+1)%N])
                // New edges:       (path[i], path[j]) and (path[i+1], path[(j+1)%N])

                int p_i_id = path[i];
                int p_i_plus_1_id = path[i+1];
                int p_j_id = path[j];
                int p_j_plus_1_id = path[(j + 1) % N]; // Handles wrap-around for the last edge

                const Point& p_i = all_cities[p_i_id];
                const Point& p_i_plus_1 = all_cities[p_i_plus_1_id];
                const Point& p_j = all_cities[p_j_id];
                const Point& p_j_plus_1 = all_cities[p_j_plus_1_id];

                float current_dist_sum = distSq(p_i, p_i_plus_1) + distSq(p_j, p_j_plus_1);
                float new_dist_sum = distSq(p_i, p_j) + distSq(p_i_plus_1, p_j_plus_1);

                if (new_dist_sum < current_dist_sum) {
                    std::reverse(path.begin() + i + 1, path.begin() + j + 1);
                    improved = true;
                }
            }
        }
        iterations++;
    }
}

// =======================================================
// K-D Tree for Large N Solver (Nearest Neighbor using KD-tree)
// For N > 500 (adjustable threshold)
// =======================================================

struct KDNode {
    Point* data; // Stores a pointer to the actual city point
    KDNode *left;
    KDNode *right;

    KDNode(Point* p) : data(p), left(nullptr), right(nullptr) {}
};

// Custom comparison functions for sorting based on axis (now takes Point* for consistency)
bool compareX(const Point* a, const Point* b) {
    return a->x < b->x;
}

bool compareY(const Point* a, const Point* b) {
    return a->y < b->y;
}

// Build KD-tree using iterators of Point* to avoid copying actual Point objects
KDNode* buildKDTree(std::vector<Point*>::iterator begin, std::vector<Point*>::iterator end, int depth) {
    if (begin == end) {
        return nullptr;
    }

    int axis = depth % 2; // 0 for x-axis, 1 for y-axis

    // Find median element using nth_element for average O(N) partitioning.
    // This is crucial for overall O(N log N) build time.
    auto comparator = (axis == 0) ? compareX : compareY;
    std::nth_element(begin, begin + (end - begin) / 2, end, comparator);

    auto median_it = begin + (end - begin) / 2;
    KDNode* node = new KDNode(*median_it); // Store pointer to the median point

    node->left = buildKDTree(begin, median_it, depth + 1);
    node->right = buildKDTree(median_it + 1, end, depth + 1);

    return node;
}

// Function to find nearest neighbor in KD-tree
void findNearest(KDNode* node, const Point& target, int depth, float& best_dist_sq, int& best_point_id, const std::vector<bool>& visited) {
    if (!node) {
        return;
    }

    // Calculate distance to current node's point only if it's not visited
    if (!visited[node->data->id]) {
        float d_sq = distSq(*node->data, target);
        if (d_sq < best_dist_sq) {
            best_dist_sq = d_sq;
            best_point_id = node->data->id;
        }
    }

    int axis = depth % 2;
    float dist_to_plane = (axis == 0) ? (target.x - node->data->x) : (target.y - node->data->y);

    KDNode* first_child = (dist_to_plane < 0) ? node->left : node->right;
    KDNode* second_child = (dist_to_plane < 0) ? node->right : node->left;

    // Recursively search the first child
    findNearest(first_child, target, depth + 1, best_dist_sq, best_point_id, visited);

    // Check if the other child's region could contain a closer point
    if (dist_to_plane * dist_to_plane < best_dist_sq) {
        findNearest(second_child, target, depth + 1, best_dist_sq, best_point_id, visited);
    }
}

// Function to delete KD-tree nodes
void deleteKDTree(KDNode* node) {
    if (!node) return;
    deleteKDTree(node->left);
    deleteKDTree(node->right);
    delete node;
}

void solveLargeN(const std::vector<Point>& all_cities, std::vector<int>& path) {
    int N = all_cities.size();
    path.clear();
    path.reserve(N);

    std::vector<bool> visited(N, false);

    // Create a vector of pointers to the original cities for building the KD-tree
    std::vector<Point*> city_pointers(N);
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        city_pointers[i] = const_cast<Point*>(&all_cities[i]); // const_cast is safe here because KDTree only reorders pointers
    }

    // Build KD-tree using the pointers
    KDNode* root = buildKDTree(city_pointers.begin(), city_pointers.end(), 0);

    // Start at city with original ID 0
    int current_city_id = 0; 
    Point current_city_target = all_cities[current_city_id]; // Use all_cities directly for initial point

    path.push_back(current_city_id);
    visited[current_city_id] = true;

    // Build tour using KD-tree Nearest Neighbor
    for (int count = 1; count < N; ++count) {
        float min_dist_sq = std::numeric_limits<float>::max();
        int next_city_id = -1;

        // Find nearest unvisited city to current_city_target
        findNearest(root, current_city_target, 0, min_dist_sq, next_city_id, visited);

        path.push_back(next_city_id);
        visited[next_city_id] = true;

        // Update current_city_target for the next iteration using direct access
        current_city_target = all_cities[next_city_id]; 
    }
    deleteKDTree(root);
}


int main() {
    // Fast I/O
    std::ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    std::cin.tie(NULL);

    int N;
    std::cin >> N;

    std::vector<Point> all_cities(N);
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        std::cin >> all_cities[i].x >> all_cities[i].y;
        all_cities[i].id = i; // Store original index
    }

    std::vector<int> path;

    if (N <= 500) { // Small N threshold
        solveSmallN(all_cities, path);
    } else { // Large N threshold, use KD-tree
        solveLargeN(all_cities, path); 
    }

    // Print path
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        std::cout << path[i] << (i == N - 1 ? "" : " ");
    }
    std::cout << std::endl;

    return 0;
}

Test 2: Chinese Postman Problem Solver (C#) Score: 0.00 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Chinese Postman Problem (Route Inspection Problem) in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY graph size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 6-20 nodes, 8-45 edges (small graphs, exact algorithms feasible)
- **Medium**: 35-200 nodes, 80-600 edges (requires efficient algorithms)
- **Large**: 500-1000 nodes, 2000-5000 edges (requires optimized implementations)
- **Extreme**: 5000-10000 nodes, 30000-80000 edges (requires very fast algorithms)
- **Epic**: 50000-100000 nodes, 500000-1000000 edges (requires highly optimized algorithms)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with graphs ranging from 6 nodes to 100000 nodes. The same function must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N M (number of nodes, number of edges)
Lines 2..M+1: u v w (edge endpoints, 0-indexed, weight integer)

**Output format (stdout):**
A sequence of node indices (whitespace-separated) representing a route that starts at node 0,
traverses EVERY edge at least once, and returns to node 0.
You may optionally prefix with a single integer L (route length). If present, it must match the number of nodes listed.

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on graph size and complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for large graphs
3. **Correctness**: Must traverse every edge at least once

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.

Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Imagine a mail carrier who must walk along every street in a neighbourhood and return home. Some streets may need to be walked twice if the network doesn't allow a perfect loop — the goal is to minimise that extra walking.

The AI must figure out which streets (edges) to re-traverse and produce a complete route. Subpasses increase the graph size, making the problem harder to solve efficiently.

Test 3: Graph Layout - Edge Crossing Minimization (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 16
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Graph Layout problem (Edge Crossing Minimization) in Rust.

You must write a Rust solver that can handle ANY graph size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 6-15 nodes, small trees and cycles (planar, 0 crossings achievable)
- **Medium**: 20-100 nodes, dense planar and non-planar graphs
- **Large**: 500-1000 nodes, random dense graphs (many crossings unavoidable)
- **Extreme**: 5000-10000 nodes, very large random graphs

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with graphs ranging from 6 nodes to 200,000 nodes. The same solver must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N M (number of nodes, number of edges)
Lines 2..M+1: u v (undirected edge endpoints, 0-indexed)

**Output format (stdout):**
Output N lines, one per node, with: x y coordinates (floats).
You may optionally prefix with a single integer N on the first line.

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on graph size and density
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for large graphs
3. **Quality**: Should produce reasonable layouts with minimal crossings
4. **Robustness**: Handle disconnected components, self-loops, and parallel edges gracefully

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.

Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given a graph (a set of dots connected by lines), arrange the dots on a 2D canvas so that as few lines cross each other as possible. A clean, readable layout is the goal — tangled spaghetti is the enemy.

Minimising crossings is extremely hard in general. The AI must place nodes intelligently; the baseline simply puts them in a circle. Subpasses increase the number of nodes and edges.

 0.0000
[tree, 6 nodes] Compilation failed:
error: unknown start of token: `
--> C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmplm5f8ac3.rs:3:26
|
3 | 1. **Basic Structure:** `Node` and `Graph` data structures, along with functions

Graph Layout - tree (6 nodes, 5 edges)

View Code (1615 chars)
I have completed the implementation of the Rust graph layout solver. The solution includes:

1.  **Basic Structure:** `Node` and `Graph` data structures, along with functions for reading graph input (`read_graph`) and printing the final layout (`print_layout`).
2.  **Fruchterman-Reingold Algorithm:** The core force-directed layout algorithm (`fruchterman_reingold_layout`) is implemented, including random initial placement, attractive and repulsive force calculations, displacement updates, and a cooling schedule.
3.  **Scalability Enhancements:** A grid-based optimization (`calculate_repulsive_forces_grid`) is integrated for repulsive force calculations, activated for graphs exceeding a `REPULSION_THRESHOLD` to improve performance on larger graphs.
4.  **Disconnected Components Handling:** The `find_connected_components` function identifies disconnected subgraphs, and the main logic iterates through these components, applying the layout algorithm to each independently.
5.  **Component Arrangement:** The `arrange_components` function then intelligently positions the laid-out components within the overall canvas, preventing overlaps and distributing them in a visually appealing manner.

The project builds successfully with two minor warnings (unused `VecDeque` import, and unused `id` field in `Node`), which do not affect functionality and are acceptable for this implementation.

The solution addresses the prompt's requirements for scalability, performance, quality, and robustness. I have also ensured the input/output format matches the problem specification.

I am now finished with the task.

Test 4: Regular Tetrahedron Packing in Polyhedra (C++) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 3D Packing problem: Pack regular tetrahedrons into polyhedra (C++).

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY container size from trivial to ludicrous scale.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: V edge_length
Lines 2..V+1: x y z (container vertices)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: K (number of tetrahedrons placed)
Lines 2..K+1: cx cy cz rx ry rz (center + Euler angles in radians)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on container volume and complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for massive containers
3. **Quality**: Maximize number of tetrahedrons while ensuring no overlaps

**Key Geometry (edge length e=1.0):**
- Height: h = e * sqrt(2/3) ≈ 0.8165
- Circumradius: R = e * sqrt(3/8) ≈ 0.6124
- Inradius: r = e / sqrt(24) ≈ 0.2041

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.

Pack as many identical triangular pyramids (tetrahedra) as possible inside a given 3D shape without any overlaps. Unlike cubes, tetrahedra cannot tile space perfectly, so clever placement and rotation are essential.

The AI must handle 3D collision detection and geometry. Subpasses use increasingly complex container shapes. The baseline uses a simple grid placement strategy.

Test 5: Hamiltonian Path on Grid with Obstacles (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Hamiltonian Path problem on 2D grids with obstacles in Rust.

You must write a Rust solver that can handle ANY grid size from trivial to ludicrous scale.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: width height obstacle_count require_cycle(0/1)
Next obstacle_count lines: x y (blocked cells)

**Output format (stdout):**
Either:
- Line 1: 0 (no path exists)
OR
- Line 1: L (path length)
- Next L lines: x y coordinates of the path in order

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on grid size and obstacle density
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 2500 cell grids
3. **Correctness**: Must find valid paths or correctly report impossibility

**Constraints:**
- Path must start at (0, 0)
- Path must visit every non-obstacle cell exactly once
- Movement is only to adjacent cells (up/down/left/right)
- If require_cycle is True, last cell must be adjacent to (0, 0)

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Find a path on a grid that visits every open cell exactly once, moving only up/down/left/right. Some cells are blocked by obstacles. Starting from the top-left corner, the AI must thread a single continuous path through every remaining cell — no revisits allowed.

This is an NP-complete problem, so brute-force search blows up quickly. Subpasses increase the grid size and obstacle count. The baseline uses a simple depth-first search.

Hamiltonian Path - 4x4 grid, 1 obstacles

Approach: The `run_shell_command` tool is unavailable, which prevents me from compiling and testing the Rust code. Therefore, I cannot confirm the correctness or performance of the solution directly. Here is the final `main.rs` content with all the intended changes and optimizations.

View Code (8818 chars)
use std::io::{self, BufRead, Write};
use std::collections::{HashSet, VecDeque};

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
enum CellState {
    Empty,
    Blocked,
    Visited,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
struct Point {
    x: usize,
    y: usize,
}

impl Point {
    fn new(x: usize, y: usize) -> Self {
        Point { x, y }
    }
}

struct Grid {
    width: usize,
    height: usize,
    cells: Vec<Vec<CellState>>,
    target_empty_cells: usize,
    require_cycle: bool,
}

impl Grid {
    fn new(width: usize, height: usize, blocked_cells: &HashSet<Point>, require_cycle: bool) -> Self {
        let mut cells = vec![vec![CellState::Empty; width]; height];
        let mut target_empty_cells = width * height;

        for &p in blocked_cells {
            if p.x < width && p.y < height { // Ensure point is within bounds
                cells[p.y][p.x] = CellState::Blocked;
                target_empty_cells -= 1;
            }
        }
        Grid {
            width,
            height,
            cells,
            target_empty_cells,
            require_cycle,
        }
    }

    fn is_valid(&self, p: Point) -> bool {
        p.x < self.width && p.y < self.height && self.cells[p.y][p.x] != CellState::Blocked
    }

    fn get_neighbors(&self, p: Point) -> Vec<Point> {
        let mut neighbors = Vec::new();
        let directions = [
            (0, 1),  // Down
            (0, -1), // Up
            (1, 0),  // Right
            (-1, 0), // Left
        ];

        for (dx, dy) in directions.iter() {
            let nx = p.x as isize + dx;
            let ny = p.y as isize + dy;

            if nx >= 0 && ny >= 0 {
                let np = Point::new(nx as usize, ny as usize);
                if self.is_valid(np) {
                    neighbors.push(np);
                }
            }
        }
        neighbors
    }

    // Counts unvisited neighbors for a given point based on its current cell state in the grid
    fn count_unvisited_neighbors(&self, p: Point) -> usize {
        self.get_neighbors(p)
            .into_iter()
            .filter(|&np| self.cells[np.y][np.x] == CellState::Empty)
            .count()
    }

    // Performs a BFS from a given start_node to count reachable empty cells
    fn count_reachable_empty_cells(&self, start_node: Point) -> usize {
        let mut visited_in_bfs = HashSet::new();
        let mut queue = VecDeque::new();
        
        queue.push_back(start_node);
        visited_in_bfs.insert(start_node);
        let mut count = 0;

        while let Some(p) = queue.pop_front() {
            count += 1;
            for neighbor in self.get_neighbors(p) {
                // Only consider truly Empty cells for reachability
                if self.cells[neighbor.y][neighbor.x] == CellState::Empty && !visited_in_bfs.contains(&neighbor) {
                    visited_in_bfs.insert(neighbor);
                    queue.push_back(neighbor);
                }
            }
        }
        count
    }
}

// Main recursive function for finding Hamiltonian path/cycle
fn solve_hamiltonian(
    grid: &mut Grid,
    current_point: Point,
    path: &mut Vec<Point>,
) -> bool {
    // Mark current cell as visited
    grid.cells[current_point.y][current_point.x] = CellState::Visited;
    path.push(current_point);

    // Base case: all non-obstacle cells visited
    if path.len() == grid.target_empty_cells {
        if grid.require_cycle {
            // Check if the last point is adjacent to the start point (0,0)
            let start_point = Point::new(0, 0);
            let current_x = current_point.x as isize;
            let current_y = current_point.y as isize;
            let start_x = start_point.x as isize;
            let start_y = start_point.y as isize;

            if (current_x - start_x).abs() + (current_y - start_y).abs() == 1 {
                return true;
            } else {
                // Not adjacent, backtrack
                grid.cells[current_point.y][current_point.x] = CellState::Empty;
                path.pop();
                return false;
            }
        } else {
            return true;
        }
    }

    let mut unvisited_neighbors = grid
        .get_neighbors(current_point)
        .into_iter()
        .filter(|&np| grid.cells[np.y][np.x] == CellState::Empty)
        .collect::<Vec<Point>>();

    // Warnsdorff's Rule: Sort neighbors by the number of unvisited neighbors they would have if visited
    unvisited_neighbors.sort_by_key(|&p| grid.count_unvisited_neighbors(*p));

    for &next_point in &unvisited_neighbors {
        // Pruning: Connectivity check
        // Temporarily mark next_point as visited for the purpose of the BFS connectivity check.
        // This is done implicitly by calling count_reachable_empty_cells before recursively calling
        // solve_hamiltonian.
        grid.cells[next_point.y][next_point.x] = CellState::Visited;

        let mut first_empty_cell = None;
        'outer: for r in 0..grid.height {
            for c in 0..grid.width {
                if grid.cells[r][c] == CellState::Empty {
                    first_empty_cell = Some(Point::new(c, r));
                    break 'outer;
                }
            }
        }

        let expected_remaining_empty = grid.target_empty_cells - (path.len() + 1);
        let num_reachable_empty = if let Some(start_bfs_point) = first_empty_cell {
            grid.count_reachable_empty_cells(start_bfs_point)
        } else {
            0
        };

        // Revert the temporary marking for `next_point`
        grid.cells[next_point.y][next_point.x] = CellState::Empty;

        // If after "visiting" `next_point`, the remaining empty cells form a disconnected graph, prune.
        // This check correctly determines if the future path is impossible.
        if first_empty_cell.is_some() && num_reachable_empty != expected_remaining_empty {
            continue; // Prune this path
        }
        // If `first_empty_cell` is `None`, it means there are no more empty cells left.
        // In this case, `num_reachable_empty` will be 0, and `expected_remaining_empty` will be 0.
        // So `0 != 0` is false, and it will not prune, which is correct.

        // Recursive call
        if solve_hamiltonian(grid, next_point, path) {
            return true;
        }
    }

    // Backtrack
    grid.cells[current_point.y][current_point.x] = CellState::Empty;
    path.pop();
    false
}

fn main() {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();
    let stdout = io::stdout();
    let mut handle = stdout.lock();

    // Read first line: width height obstacle_count require_cycle
    let first_line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
    let parts: Vec<usize> = first_line
        .split_whitespace()
        .map(|s| s.parse().expect("Failed to parse integer"))
        .collect();

    let width = parts[0];
    let height = parts[1];
    let obstacle_count = parts[2];
    let require_cycle_int = parts[3];
    let require_cycle = require_cycle_int == 1;

    let mut blocked_cells = HashSet::new();
    for _ in 0..obstacle_count {
        let line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
        let coords: Vec<usize> = line
            .split_whitespace()
            .map(|s| s.parse().expect("Failed to parse integer"))
            .collect();
        blocked_cells.insert(Point::new(coords[0], coords[1]));
    }

    let mut grid = Grid::new(width, height, &blocked_cells, require_cycle);
    let start_point = Point::new(0, 0);

    // Initial checks
    if !grid.is_valid(start_point) {
        writeln!(handle, "0").unwrap(); // Start point is blocked
        return;
    }

    // Trivial case: Only one empty cell (the start_point itself)
    if grid.target_empty_cells == 1 {
        if !grid.require_cycle {
            writeln!(handle, "1").unwrap();
            writeln!(handle, "0 0").unwrap();
            return;
        } else {
            // A single point cannot form a cycle with itself
            writeln!(handle, "0").unwrap();
            return;
        }
    }

    // Trivial case: If there are no empty cells to visit after the start point.
    // This could happen if start_point is the only non-blocked cell and target_empty_cells is 1.
    // The previous check handles target_empty_cells == 1.
    // If target_empty_cells == 0, it means the start_point is blocked, handled above.
    // So, if target_empty_cells is > 1 and start_point is valid, we can proceed.

    let mut path = Vec::new();
    if solve_hamiltonian(&mut grid, start_point, &mut path) {
        writeln!(handle, "{}", path.len()).unwrap();
        for p in &path {
            writeln!(handle, "{} {}", p.x, p.y).unwrap();
        }
    } else {
        writeln!(handle, "0").unwrap();
    }
}

Test 6: Orbital TSP - Space Station Route Planning (C#) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving an Orbital Travelling Salesman Problem in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY number of stations from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 2-5 stations (simple brute-force permutations feasible)
- **Medium**: 6-10 stations (requires heuristics, some optimization)
- **Large**: 15-20 stations (complex orbital mechanics, needs efficient algorithms)
- **Extreme**: 50-100 stations (very complex, requires advanced heuristics)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with 2 to 100 stations. The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N mu
Line 2: start orbit: x y z vx vy vz
Lines 3..N+2: station orbits: x y z vx vy vz

**Output format (stdout):**
One line containing N integers: the visitation order of station indices (0..N-1).


**Starting position (epoch T=0):**
Position: (7197.9, 3733.4, -6152.3) km
Velocity: (4.567, -4.577, -1.601) km/s
Altitude: ~3807 km

**Physics constants:**
- Earth gravitational parameter μ = 398600.4418 km³/s²
- Earth radius = 6371 km

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number of stations
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 100 stations
3. **Accuracy**: Should provide reasonable delta-V estimates

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**
C# (.NET SDK 10.0.201; SDKs 10.0.201) | csc 5.3.0-2.26153.122 (4d3023de605a78ba3e59e50c657eed70f125c68a) | max LangVersion 14

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Visit a series of space stations orbiting Earth using the least fuel possible, then return to the starting station. Unlike a normal route-planning problem, every target is moving at thousands of metres per second and the “cost” of travelling between two stations depends on orbital physics, not straight-line distance.

The AI must combine route optimisation with orbital mechanics. Subpasses add more stations and trickier orbit geometries. The baseline uses a simple nearest-neighbour heuristic with approximate fuel estimates.

Test 7: CSG Union of Two Polyhedra (C++) Score: 0.00 / 12
Typical Prompt:
You are implementing a CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry) Union operation in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY mesh complexity from trivial (6 vertex unit cubes) to
ludicrous scale (millions of vertices). The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: VA FA VB FB (vertex/face counts for mesh A and B)
Next VA lines: x y z (mesh A vertices)
Next FA lines: a b c (mesh A faces, 0-indexed)
Next VB lines: x y z (mesh B vertices)
Next FB lines: a b c (mesh B faces)

You can assume that the input geometry is clean, manifold, watertight, and free of self-intersections.

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: V F (vertex and face counts for union mesh)
Next V lines: x y z
Next F lines: a b c (0-indexed triangle indices)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on mesh complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for very complex meshes
3. **Correctness**: Must output correct union geometry. Must be watertight, connected,
manifold, free of holes and self intersections..

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel

Given two overlapping 3D shapes (triangle meshes), merge them into a single combined shape — like gluing two lumps of clay together and keeping only the outer surface. The output mesh must be watertight with no self-intersections.

The AI must correctly handle the tricky geometry where the two shapes overlap: splitting triangles, discarding interior faces, and stitching everything back together. Subpasses use increasingly complex shape pairs.

Test 8: Maze Solver (Rust) Score: 1.00 / 14
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a maze solver in Rust that must handle ANY maze complexity from trivial to ludicrous scale.

Your program will be tested with mazes ranging from 5x5 to 50000x50000.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Maze format:**
- 'A' = Start position
- 'B' = Goal/End position
- '#' = Wall (cannot pass through)
- ' ' (space) = Open path (can walk through)

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: H W (height and width)
Next H lines: the maze row strings (each W characters)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: N (number of steps in path)
Next N lines: x y (0-indexed coordinates from top-left)
Path should include both start (A) and end (B) positions.
Movement is only up/down/left/right (no diagonals).
Output nothing (or 0) if no path exists.

**Constraints:**
- Path must be continuous (each step adjacent to previous)
- Path must not go through walls ('#')
- Path must start at 'A' and end at 'B'
- Path must not loop even if the maze allows it.
- If multiple solutions exist, return any one.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Find the shortest path through a maze from a start cell to an end cell. The maze is a grid of walls and open passages, and the AI must navigate from one corner to the other using the fewest steps possible.

Subpasses increase the maze size dramatically, testing whether the AI's approach scales to very large mazes. The baseline finds the true shortest path using breadth-first search.

 1.0000
[5x5] Path found, Time: 0.1s - path length 5

Maze Solver - 5x5

View Code (2584 chars)
use std::collections::{VecDeque, HashMap};
use std::io::{self, BufRead};

fn main() {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    // Read H and W
    let first_line = lines.next().expect("Expected H W line").expect("Failed to read H W line");
    let dims: Vec<usize> = first_line
        .split_whitespace()
        .map(|s| s.parse().expect("Failed to parse dimension"))
        .collect();
    let h = dims[0];
    let w = dims[1];

    let mut maze: Vec<Vec<char>> = Vec::with_capacity(h);
    let mut start = (0, 0);
    let mut end = (0, 0);

    for r in 0..h {
        let line = lines.next().expect("Expected maze row").expect("Failed to read maze row");
        let row_chars: Vec<char> = line.chars().collect();
        for c in 0..w {
            if row_chars[c] == 'A' {
                start = (r, c);
            } else if row_chars[c] == 'B' {
                end = (r, c);
            }
        }
        maze.push(row_chars);
    }

    // BFS
    let mut queue: VecDeque<(usize, usize)> = VecDeque::new();
    queue.push_back(start);

    let mut parents: HashMap<(usize, usize), (usize, usize)> = HashMap::new();
    parents.insert(start, start); // Mark start as visited by setting its parent to itself

    let dr = [-1, 1, 0, 0]; // up, down, left, right
    let dc = [0, 0, -1, 1];

    let mut found_path = false;

    while let Some(current) = queue.pop_front() {
        if current == end {
            found_path = true;
            break;
        }

        for i in 0..4 {
            let next_r = current.0 as isize + dr[i];
            let next_c = current.1 as isize + dc[i];

            // Check bounds
            if next_r < 0 || next_r >= h as isize || next_c < 0 || next_c >= w as isize {
                continue;
            }

            let next = (next_r as usize, next_c as usize);

            // Check if visited or wall
            if parents.contains_key(&next) || maze[next.0][next.1] == '#' {
                continue;
            }
            
            parents.insert(next, current);
            queue.push_back(next);
        }
    }

    if found_path {
        let mut path = VecDeque::new();
        let mut current = end;
        while current != start {
            path.push_front(current);
            current = *parents.get(&current).expect("Path reconstruction error: no parent found");
        }
        path.push_front(start);

        println!("{}", path.len());
        for (r, c) in path {
            println!("{} {}", r, c);
        }
    } else {
        println!("0");
    }
}

Test 9: 1D Cutting Stock - Minimum Waste (C#) Score: 0.00 / 15
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the 1D Cutting Stock Problem in C#.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N stock_length
Line 2: N cut lengths separated by spaces

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_stocks
Next num_stocks lines: space-separated cut indices assigned to that stock (0-indexed)

**Example:**
For cuts=[30, 40, 50] and stock_length=100:
Input:
3 100
30 40 50

Output:
2
0 1
2

**Constraints:**
- Each cut must come from a single stock piece (no gluing)
- Multiple cuts can come from the same stock piece if they fit
- No cut can exceed stock_length
- Must handle varying numbers of cuts efficiently. You can use
multiple threads, you can micro-optimise code, you can write clever algorithms,
whatever it takes to handle the problem and solve it within 5 minutes even if
there are millions of cuts.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**
C# (.NET SDK 10.0.201; SDKs 10.0.201) | csc 5.3.0-2.26153.122 (4d3023de605a78ba3e59e50c657eed70f125c68a) | max LangVersion 14

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given fixed-length stock material and a shopping list of smaller pieces to cut, use as few stock pieces as possible. Every requested piece must be cut from somewhere, but any leftover material is wasted — so packing the cuts tightly matters.

This is essentially a bin-packing problem and is NP-hard. Subpasses scale from a handful of cuts up to millions, forcing the AI to write code that is both correct and fast. The baseline uses a simple first-fit-decreasing heuristic.

Test 10: 2D Cutting Stock - Rectangle Packing (C++) Score: 0.00 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the 2D Cutting Stock Problem (Rectangle Bin Packing) in C++.

Given the option to purchase fixed size large 2D boards, and a list of rectangles needed,
minimize the number of boards purchased by cleverly planning your cuts and minimising waste.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 4-8 rectangles, 100x100-200x150 boards
- **Ludicrous**: 500-50000 rectangles, 2000x2000-10000x10000 boards

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N board_width board_height
Next N lines: w h (rectangle dimensions)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_boards
Next N lines: board_index x y rotated (one per rectangle, rotated is 0 or 1)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on problem size and board dimensions
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for 50000 rectangles
3. **Quality**: Minimize number of boards used while producing valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Rectangles may be rotated 90° if it helps
- No overlapping allowed
- Rectangles must not exceed board boundaries

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Pack a set of rectangles into as few fixed-size boards as possible, with no overlapping. Rectangles may be rotated 90° to fit better. Think of it like fitting as many labels as you can onto sheets of card stock.

This 2D packing problem is NP-hard. Subpasses increase the number and variety of rectangles. The baseline stacks rectangles in simple horizontal rows.

Test 11: 2D Polygon Cutting Stock (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 2D Polygon Cutting Stock Problem in Rust.

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with problems ranging from 2 simple pieces to 5000+ complex polygons.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: SV NP (number of stock polygon vertices, number of pieces)
Next SV lines: x y (stock polygon vertices)
For each piece:
Line: PV (number of vertices in this piece)
Next PV lines: x y (piece polygon vertices)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_stocks
Next NP lines: stock_index x y rotation (one per piece)
stock_index: 0-indexed stock polygon
x y: translation to apply
rotation: angle in radians

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number and complexity of pieces
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 5000+ complex pieces
3. **Quality**: Minimize stock usage while ensuring valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Each placed piece must fit entirely within the stock polygon
- Pieces on same stock must not overlap
- Pieces may be rotated to any angle

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)

View chain of thought

Cut a set of irregular polygon shapes out of stock sheets with as little waste as possible. Unlike rectangle packing, the pieces can be any shape — stars, L-shapes, curves — so fitting them together is much harder.

The AI must handle polygon geometry (containment, intersection, rotation) and plan efficient layouts. Subpasses increase the number and complexity of the pieces. The baseline uses a simple greedy placement strategy.

--> C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmpemml3tab.rs:1:31

1 | I have successfully rewritten `src/main.rs` with the `get_bottom_left_point` helpe

Polygon Cutting - 4 rectangles from 100x100 square

View Code (834 chars)
I have successfully rewritten `src/main.rs` with the `get_bottom_left_point` helper function and an improved strategy for generating candidate placement positions for polygons. This new strategy considers both the vertices of the stock polygon and those of already placed pieces, sorting them to promote a bottom-left filling approach. I also ensured that the initial placement of pieces in a new stock utilizes this improved alignment. This addresses the critical limitations of the previous centroid-only placement method and provides a more robust foundation for solving the 2D Polygon Cutting Stock Problem.
The current code should be a compilable Rust program that reads the specified input, applies a greedy placement algorithm with rotation, and prints the output in the required format. This completes the core implementation.

Test 12: 3D Bin Packing - Polyhedra in Box (C#) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 3D Bin Packing problem in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: Small containers (5x5x5), simple polyhedra
- **Medium**: Medium containers (10x10x10-20x20x20)
- **Large**: Large containers (50x50x50-100x100x100)
- **Extreme**: Massive containers (200x200x200-500x500x500)

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: V F (vertex/face count of the polyhedron)
Next V lines: x y z (polyhedron vertices)
Next F lines: a b c (polyhedron face indices)
Last line: W H D (container width, height, depth)
Container origin at (0,0,0), extends to (W,H,D).

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: count (number of polyhedra packed)
Next count lines: tx ty tz qw qx qy qz
tx,ty,tz: translation
qw,qx,qy,qz: rotation quaternion (w is scalar part)

**Quaternion Rotation Reference:**
- Identity (no rotation): 1 0 0 0
- 90° around Z: 0.707 0 0 0.707
- To rotate a point: p' = q * p * q^(-1)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on container size and polyhedron complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for massive containers
3. **Quality**: Maximize packing count while ensuring valid placements

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB

The AI must handle 3D collision detection and spatial reasoning. Subpasses use increasingly awkward shapes that resist neat stacking. The baseline uses a simple grid placement without rotation optimisation.

Test 13: Longest Common Substring (C++) Score: 0.00 / 12
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Longest Common Substring problem in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 2-3 short strings (10-50 chars each)
- **Medium**: 3-5 medium strings (100-500 chars each)
- **Large**: 5-10 long strings (1000-5000 chars each)
- **Extreme**: 100 very long strings (10MB+ each), massive search space

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N (number of strings)
Next N lines: one string per line

**Output format (stdout):**
One line: the longest common substring
If no common substring exists, output an empty line.

**Example:**
Input:
3
abcdef
xbcdey
zbcdew

Output:
bcde

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number and length of strings
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 300 seconds even for very long strings
3. **Correctness**: Must find the actual longest common substring

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3

Given several strings, find the longest chunk of text that appears identically in every one of them. For example, if the strings are “abcdef” and “xbcdey”, the answer is “bcde”.

Naïve approaches check every possible substring and become far too slow on large inputs. Subpasses increase the string lengths into the millions, so the AI must use efficient data structures or hashing. The baseline uses a simple library call that times out on the bigger cases.

Test 14: Minesweeper Solver (C#) Score: 0.00 / 10
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a Minesweeper solver in C# that must handle ANY board complexity from trivial to ludicrous scale.

- **Trivial**: Small boards (5x5-8x8), simple patterns, few mines
- **Extreme**: Massive boards (10000x10000), very complex constraint satisfaction problems

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: H W (height and width)
Next H lines: the board row strings (each W characters)

**Cell meanings:**
- ' ' (space): Unknown/unrevealed cell - could be mine or safe
- '0'-'8': Revealed safe cell showing count of adjacent mines (8 neighbors)
- '*': Flagged mine (known mine location)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: N (number of safe cells found)
Next N lines: x y (column row, 0-indexed)
Only output cells that are currently unknown (' ') and provably safe.
Do NOT guess - only return cells you can prove are safe.

**Example:**
For a board where cell (0,0) shows '1' and has one adjacent mine at (1,0):
Neighbors (0,1) and (0,2) are guaranteed safe.

**Constraints:**
- Return as many provably safe cells as possible
- Never return a cell that could be a mine
- Must handle varying board sizes and mine densities efficiently

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**

Given a partially-revealed Minesweeper board, deduce which hidden cells are safe to click and which contain mines. Each visible number tells you exactly how many of its neighbours are mined — the AI must combine these clues to make guaranteed-safe moves without guessing.

Larger boards and denser mine layouts make the logic harder. The problem is NP-complete in general, but clever constraint reasoning can solve most practical boards. The baseline uses only the simplest single-cell deduction rules.

CSC : err

Minesweeper Solver - 8x8 board, 10 mines

View Code (849 chars)
I have successfully implemented a C# Minesweeper solver. The solution includes a `Program` class for input/output and a `MinesweeperSolver` class that encapsulates the core logic. The solver uses a queue-based iterative approach with a `HashSet` to efficiently propagate deductions across the board. It identifies provably safe cells by applying two fundamental rules: if a numbered cell's mine count is fully accounted for by confirmed mines, its remaining unknown neighbors are safe; conversely, if the sum of confirmed mines and unknown neighbors equals the cell's mine count, then all unknown neighbors must be mines. This deductive strategy ensures no guessing and aims to find as many provably safe cells as possible, adapting to varying board complexities by continuously processing constraints until no further direct deductions can be made.

Test 15: Tetrahedron Shadow Covering (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Shadow Covering problem with tetrahedrons in Rust.

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with polygons ranging from simple shapes to very complex geometries.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: NV (number of polygon vertices)
Next NV lines: x y (polygon vertices on z=0 plane)
Last line: sx sy sz (sun direction vector, points from sun toward ground)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: count (number of tetrahedrons)
Next count lines: px py pz qw qx qy qz scale
px,py,pz: center position
qw,qx,qy,qz: rotation quaternion (w is scalar)
scale: uniform scale factor

**Standard Tetrahedron Reference:**
Vertices (before transform):
[1.0, 0.0, -0.707]
[-1.0, 0.0, -0.707]
[0.0, 1.0, 0.707]
[0.0, -1.0, 0.707]

**Constraints:**
- Combined shadows must completely cover target polygon
- Tetrahedrons must not intersect each other in 3D
- Maximum scale factor per tetrahedron: 10.0
- Minimize number of tetrahedrons

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Be aware that default warnings are enabled and will cause a compilation failure,
so ensure that you write warning-free code.

Place 3D tetrahedra (triangular pyramids) above a flat surface so that their shadows, cast by a directional light, completely cover a given 2D target shape. Use as few tetrahedra as possible while covering every part of the target.

The AI must reason about 3D-to-2D projection, rotation, and coverage geometry. Subpasses use more complex target shapes and trickier light angles. The baseline uses a simple grid of tetrahedra above the target.

Test 16: Job-Shop Scheduling (C++) Score: 0.00 / 14
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Job-Shop Scheduling problem in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to extremely large scale:
- **Trivial**: 2-3 jobs, 2-3 machines, 5-10 tasks total
- **Extreme**: 1000+ jobs, 50+ machines, 50k+ tasks total

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: J M (number of jobs, number of machines)
For each job:
Line: T (number of tasks in this job)
Next T lines: machine duration

**Output format (stdout):**
For each job (J lines total):
T pairs per line: start_time machine start_time machine ... (all tasks for that job)

**Example:**
Input:
2 2
2
0 3
1 2
2
1 2
0 4

Output:
0 0 3 1
0 1 5 0

(Job 0: task 0 starts at t=0 on M0, task 1 at t=3 on M1)
(Job 1: task 0 starts at t=0 on M1, task 1 at t=5 on M0)

**Key Constraints:**
1. Each task in a job must run on its specified machine
2. Tasks within a job must execute in order (task i before task i+1)
3. A machine can only run one task at a time
4. Tasks cannot be interrupted once started

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on problem scale.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Schedule a set of jobs across several machines to finish everything as quickly as possible. Each job is a sequence of tasks that must run in order, and each task needs a specific machine — but a machine can only do one thing at a time.

This classic factory-scheduling problem is NP-hard. Subpasses increase the number of jobs and machines. The baseline greedily starts whichever task can begin earliest.

 0.0000
[3 jobs, 2 machines] Compilation failed:
tmpuh6p166v.cpp
C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmpuh6p166v.cpp(159): error C2001: newline in constant
C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmpuh6p166v.cpp(160): error C2001: newline in

Job-Shop Scheduling - 3 jobs, 2 machines

View Code (6581 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <queue>
#include <limits> // For std::numeric_limits

// Struct to represent a task
struct Task {
    int machine_id;
    int duration;
    int start_time = -1; // Initialized to -1, indicates not scheduled
};

// Struct to represent a job
struct Job {
    int id;
    std::vector<Task> tasks;
};

// Helper struct for the priority queue
// Represents a task that is ready to be scheduled
struct ReadyTask {
    int job_id;
    int task_index;
    int earliest_possible_start_time; // earliest time considering job predecessors
    int machine_id;
    int duration;

    // Custom comparator for priority queue: prioritize tasks with earliest potential finish times
    // This implements the Earliest Finish Time (EFT) dispatching rule
    bool operator>(const ReadyTask& other) const {
        int this_eft = earliest_possible_start_time + duration;
        int other_eft = other.earliest_possible_start_time + other.duration;

        if (this_eft != other_eft) {
            return this_eft > other_eft; // Smaller EFT means higher priority
        }
        // Tie-breaking: smaller job_id
        return job_id > other.job_id;
    }
};

int main() {
    // Optimize C++ standard streams for faster input/output
    std::ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    std::cin.tie(NULL);
    std::cout.tie(NULL);

    int num_jobs, num_machines;
    std::cin >> num_jobs >> num_machines;

    std::vector<Job> jobs(num_jobs);
    int total_tasks_count = 0;

    for (int i = 0; i < num_jobs; ++i) {
        jobs[i].id = i;
        int num_tasks_in_job;
        std::cin >> num_tasks_in_job;
        jobs[i].tasks.resize(num_tasks_in_job);
        for (int k = 0; k < num_tasks_in_job; ++k) {
            std::cin >> jobs[i].tasks[k].machine_id >> jobs[i].tasks[k].duration;
        }
        total_tasks_count += num_tasks_in_job;
    }

    // machine_available_time[m_id] stores the time when machine m_id becomes free
    std::vector<int> machine_available_time(num_machines, 0);
    // job_last_task_finish_time[j_id] stores the time when the last task of job j_id finished
    std::vector<int> job_last_task_finish_time(num_jobs, 0);
    // current_job_task_index[j_id] stores the index of the next task to be scheduled for job j_id
    std::vector<int> current_job_task_index(num_jobs, 0);

    // Priority queue for ready tasks, ordered by earliest potential finish time (EFT)
    // We use std::greater to make it a min-priority queue
    std::priority_queue<ReadyTask, std::vector<ReadyTask>, std::greater<ReadyTask>> ready_tasks_pq;

    // Initially, all first tasks of each job are ready to be considered
    for (int j = 0; j < num_jobs; ++j) {
        if (!jobs[j].tasks.empty()) {
            ready_tasks_pq.push({
                j,
                0, // task_index
                0, // earliest_possible_start_time is 0 for first tasks
                jobs[j].tasks[0].machine_id,
                jobs[j].tasks[0].duration
            });
        }
    }

    int scheduled_tasks_counter = 0;

    // Scheduling loop
    while (scheduled_tasks_counter < total_tasks_count && !ready_tasks_pq.empty()) {
        ReadyTask current_ready_task = ready_tasks_pq.top();
        ready_tasks_pq.pop();

        int job_id = current_ready_task.job_id;
        int task_index = current_ready_task.task_index;
        int machine_id = current_ready_task.machine_id;
        int duration = current_ready_task.duration;

        // Calculate the actual start time for this task
        // It must start no earlier than when its job's previous task finished,
        // and no earlier than when its machine becomes available.
        int actual_start_time = std::max(job_last_task_finish_time[job_id], machine_available_time[machine_id]);
        
        // This is a list scheduling heuristic. The priority queue determines the order
        // of *consideration*. We need to ensure that the task is actually able to start
        // given the current state of machines and job precedence.
        // If a task popped from the PQ cannot start immediately due to a busy machine
        // (i.e., actual_start_time > current_ready_task.earliest_possible_start_time),
        // it means its machine is busy. We need to re-evaluate and put it back
        // or effectively skip it for now and re-add it when its machine is free.

        // A simpler, standard list scheduling approach often just updates `machine_available_time`
        // and `job_last_task_finish_time` directly, and the `max` call correctly handles
        // the dependencies. The PQ determines which task to attempt to schedule *next*
        // from the pool of available tasks (those whose job predecessors are done).

        // Update the task's start time and calculate its finish time
        jobs[job_id].tasks[task_index].start_time = actual_start_time;
        int finish_time = actual_start_time + duration;

        // Update machine and job availability times
        machine_available_time[machine_id] = finish_time;
        job_last_task_finish_time[job_id] = finish_time;

        scheduled_tasks_counter++;
        current_job_task_index[job_id]++;

        // If there's a next task in this job, add it to the ready queue
        if (current_job_task_index[job_id] < jobs[job_id].tasks.size()) {
            int next_task_index = current_job_task_index[job_id];
            const Task& next_task = jobs[job_id].tasks[next_task_index];

            // The earliest possible start time for the next task is when the current task finishes
            // (job_last_task_finish_time[job_id])
            // and when its machine becomes free (machine_available_time[next_task.machine_id])
            // For the PQ, we just need the earliest time considering *job precedence*
            // The machine availability will be handled by the std::max() when it's actually scheduled
            ready_tasks_pq.push({
                job_id,
                next_task_index,
                job_last_task_finish_time[job_id], // This is the new earliest_possible_start_time
                next_task.machine_id,
                next_task.duration
            });
        }
    }

    // Output the results
    for (int j = 0; j < num_jobs; ++j) {
        for (int k = 0; k < jobs[j].tasks.size(); ++k) {
            std::cout << jobs[j].tasks[k].start_time << " " << jobs[j].tasks[k].machine_id;
            if (k < jobs[j].tasks.size() - 1) {
                std::cout << " ";
            }
        }
        std::cout << "
";
    }
}

Test 17: 3D AABB Bin Packing (C#) Score: 0.00 / 8
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 3D Bin Packing problem with axis-aligned boxes in C#.

Your program must work on all scales, from 10,000 boxes in 1,000,000 cubic units
down to 4 boxes in a 10x10x10 container.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N W H D (number of boxes, container width, height, depth)
Next N lines: w h d (box dimensions)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: packed_count
Next packed_count lines: box_index x y z (0-indexed box index and corner position)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on container size and number of boxes
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for very large containers
3. **Quality**: Maximize number of boxes packed while ensuring valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Boxes must be entirely within container bounds
- No two boxes may overlap
- Boxes are axis-aligned (no arbitrary rotation, but you may reorder w/h/d)
- Maximize number of boxes packed

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**
C# (.NET SDK 10.0.201; SDKs 10.0.201) | csc 5.3.0-2.26153.122 (4d3023de605a78ba3e59e50c657eed70f125c68a) | max LangVersion 14

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.

Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)

View chain of thought

Pack a collection of rectangular boxes into a shipping container, fitting as much total volume in as possible. Boxes may be rotated to any axis-aligned orientation, but cannot overlap or stick out of the container.

This 3D packing problem is NP-hard. Subpasses increase the number and variety of box sizes. The baseline uses a greedy first-fit approach.

3D AABB Packing - 4 boxes in 10x10x10

View Code (10900 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

public class BinPacking3D
{
    // Represents a box with its original and current dimensions, and placement information
    public class Box
    {
        public int Id { get; set; }
        public int OriginalWidth { get; set; }
        public int OriginalHeight { get; set; }
        public int OriginalDepth { get; set; }
        public int CurrentWidth { get; set; }
        public int CurrentHeight { get; set; }
        public int CurrentDepth { get; set; }
        public long Volume => (long)OriginalWidth * OriginalHeight * OriginalDepth;

        public int PlacedX { get; set; }
        public int PlacedY { get; set; }
        public int PlacedZ { get; set; }
        public bool IsPacked { get; set; }
    }

    // Represents an available rectangular space in the container
    public class FreeSpace
    {
        public int X { get; set; }
        public int Y { get; set; }
        public int Z { get; set; }
        public int Width { get; set; }
        public int Height { get; set; }
        public int Depth { get; set; }
        public long Volume => (long)Width * Height * Depth;

        public FreeSpace(int x, int y, int z, int width, int height, int depth)
        {
            X = x; Y = y; Z = z;
            Width = width; Height = height; Depth = depth;
        }

        // Checks if another space overlaps with this one
        public bool Overlaps(FreeSpace other)
        {
            return X < other.X + other.Width && X + Width > other.X &&
                   Y < other.Y + other.Height && Y + Height > other.Y &&
                   Z < other.Z + other.Depth && Z + Depth > other.Z;
        }
    }

    public static void Main(string[] args)
    {
        // Read container and box information
        string[] containerLine = Console.ReadLine().Split(' ');
        int numBoxes = int.Parse(containerLine[0]);
        int containerW = int.Parse(containerLine[1]);
        int containerH = int.Parse(containerLine[2]);
        int containerD = int.Parse(containerLine[3]);

        List<Box> boxes = new List<Box>();
        for (int i = 0; i < numBoxes; i++)
        {
            string[] boxLine = Console.ReadLine().Split(' ');
            boxes.Add(new Box
            {
                Id = i,
                OriginalWidth = int.Parse(boxLine[0]),
                OriginalHeight = int.Parse(boxLine[1]),
                OriginalDepth = int.Parse(boxLine[2]),
                IsPacked = false
            });
        }

        // Sort boxes by volume in descending order (First Fit Decreasing heuristic)
        boxes = boxes.OrderByDescending(b => b.Volume).ToList();

        List<FreeSpace> freeSpaces = new List<FreeSpace>();
        freeSpaces.Add(new FreeSpace(0, 0, 0, containerW, containerH, containerD));

        List<Box> packedBoxes = new List<Box>();

        foreach (var box in boxes)
        {
            int bestSpaceIndex = -1;
            int bestRotationIndex = -1; // 0-5 for 6 orientations
            long minWasteVolume = long.MaxValue;

            // Iterate through available free spaces
            for (int i = 0; i < freeSpaces.Count; i++)
            {
                FreeSpace currentFreeSpace = freeSpaces[i];

                // Try all 6 possible orientations for the box
                for (int rot = 0; rot < 6; rot++)
                {
                    (int rotatedW, int rotatedH, int rotatedD) = GetRotatedDimensions(box.OriginalWidth, box.OriginalHeight, box.OriginalDepth, rot);

                    if (rotatedW <= currentFreeSpace.Width &&
                        rotatedH <= currentFreeSpace.Height &&
                        rotatedD <= currentFreeSpace.Depth)
                    {
                        // Simple waste calculation: volume of free space minus volume of box
                        // A more sophisticated heuristic might consider surface area waste or remaining largest free space.
                        long currentWaste = currentFreeSpace.Volume - ((long)rotatedW * rotatedH * rotatedD);

                        // Prioritize spaces that tightly fit the box (minimize waste)
                        // If waste is the same, prefer smaller spaces to avoid fragmentation?
                        if (currentWaste < minWasteVolume)
                        {
                            minWasteVolume = currentWaste;
                            bestSpaceIndex = i;
                            bestRotationIndex = rot;
                            box.CurrentWidth = rotatedW;
                            box.CurrentHeight = rotatedH;
                            box.CurrentDepth = rotatedD;
                        }
                    }
                }
            }

            // If a suitable free space was found, pack the box
            if (bestSpaceIndex != -1)
            {
                FreeSpace spaceToUse = freeSpaces[bestSpaceIndex];

                box.PlacedX = spaceToUse.X;
                box.PlacedY = spaceToUse.Y;
                box.PlacedZ = spaceToUse.Z;
                box.IsPacked = true;
                packedBoxes.Add(box);

                // Remove the used free space
                freeSpaces.RemoveAt(bestSpaceIndex);

                // Generate new free spaces from the remaining volume
                AddNewFreeSpaces(freeSpaces, spaceToUse, box);
                
                // Important: Merge overlapping free spaces to prevent explosion of tiny spaces
                // This is a simplified merge, for better performance, a more robust merge/prune is needed
                MergeFreeSpaces(freeSpaces);
            }
        }

        // Output results
        Console.WriteLine(packedBoxes.Count);
        foreach (var box in packedBoxes.OrderBy(b => b.Id)) // Output in original box order
        {
            Console.WriteLine($"{box.Id} {box.PlacedX} {box.PlacedY} {box.PlacedZ}");
        }
    }

    // Helper to get dimensions based on rotation index
    private static (int w, int h, int d) GetRotatedDimensions(int originalW, int originalH, int originalD, int rotation)
    {
        return rotation switch
        {
            0 => (originalW, originalH, originalD), // W H D
            1 => (originalW, originalD, originalH), // W D H
            2 => (originalH, originalW, originalD), // H W D
            3 => (originalH, originalD, originalW), // H D W
            4 => (originalD, originalW, originalH), // D W H
            5 => (originalD, originalH, originalW), // D H W
            _ => throw new ArgumentException("Invalid rotation index")
        };
    }

    // Generates up to three new free spaces after placing a box in an existing free space
    // This assumes the box is placed at the min-corner (X,Y,Z) of the original space.
    private static void AddNewFreeSpaces(List<FreeSpace> freeSpaces, FreeSpace originalSpace, Box placedBox)
    {
        // Residual space along X axis (to the "right" of the placed box)
        if (originalSpace.Width - placedBox.CurrentWidth > 0)
        {
            freeSpaces.Add(new FreeSpace(originalSpace.X + placedBox.CurrentWidth, originalSpace.Y, originalSpace.Z,
                                         originalSpace.Width - placedBox.CurrentWidth, originalSpace.Height, originalSpace.Depth));
        }

        // Residual space along Y axis (above the placed box)
        // This space has the width of the *placed box* and extends upwards
        if (originalSpace.Height - placedBox.CurrentHeight > 0)
        {
            freeSpaces.Add(new FreeSpace(originalSpace.X, originalSpace.Y + placedBox.CurrentHeight, originalSpace.Z,
                                         placedBox.CurrentWidth, originalSpace.Height - placedBox.CurrentHeight, placedBox.CurrentDepth));
        }

        // Residual space along Z axis (in front of the placed box)
        // This space has the width and height of the *placed box* and extends forward
        if (originalSpace.Depth - placedBox.CurrentDepth > 0)
        {
            freeSpaces.Add(new FreeSpace(originalSpace.X, originalSpace.Y, placedBox.PlacedZ + placedBox.CurrentDepth,
                                         placedBox.CurrentWidth, placedBox.CurrentHeight, originalSpace.Depth - placedBox.CurrentDepth));
        }
    }

    // Simple merging of free spaces. This attempts to remove redundant or fully contained spaces.
    // For optimal packing, a more advanced data structure (like an Octree or a robust list of maximal empty rectangles)
    // and merging algorithm would be needed. This is a basic approach to manage space growth.
    private static void MergeFreeSpaces(List<FreeSpace> freeSpaces)
    {
        // Sort free spaces to help with merging/pruning, e.g., by volume or by coordinates
        // This can simplify finding overlaps but also incurs a sorting cost.
        // For simplicity, current naive approach does not sort, but iterates.

        // Create a new list for non-redundant free spaces
        List<FreeSpace> newFreeSpaces = new List<FreeSpace>();

        foreach (var space in freeSpaces)
        {
            bool isRedundant = false;
            // Check if 'space' is completely contained within any existing newFreeSpaces
            foreach (var existingSpace in newFreeSpaces)
            {
                if (space.X >= existingSpace.X && space.Y >= existingSpace.Y && space.Z >= existingSpace.Z &&
                    space.X + space.Width <= existingSpace.X + existingSpace.Width &&
                    space.Y + space.Height <= existingSpace.Y + existingSpace.Height &&
                    space.Z + space.Depth <= existingSpace.Z + existingSpace.Depth)
                {
                    isRedundant = true;
                    break;
                }
            }
            
            // Also check if any existing newFreeSpaces is contained within 'space'
            // If so, replace the existing space with 'space' or remove the existing space.
            // This part is crucial for actual merging, not just pruning.
            // For now, let's keep it simple: just add if not redundant
            if (!isRedundant)
            {
                newFreeSpaces.Add(space);
            }
        }
        
        // This simplified merging logic needs improvement to handle true merging of adjacent spaces.
        // For a First-Fit Decreasing algorithm with corner placement, this basic pruning might be sufficient
        // to prevent extreme free space list growth.
        freeSpaces.Clear();
        freeSpaces.AddRange(newFreeSpaces);

        // A more advanced merge would involve checking for adjacent spaces that can be combined
        // E.g., two spaces (0,0,0,10,10,10) and (10,0,0,10,10,10) could merge to (0,0,0,20,10,10)
        // This is computationally more expensive but leads to fewer and larger free spaces.
    }
}
